meta.note: Guides reuse the taxonomy in tools.json (useCategories, pedagogyFrameworks, roles, bands, subjects). Only guide-specific axes live here.

difficulties.label: Starter
difficulties.description: No prior AI experience needed. Follow along step by step.

difficulties.label: Confident
difficulties.description: You've used AI a little and want to do something more deliberate.

difficulties.label: Advanced
difficulties.description: You're comfortable prompting and want to refine technique.

guides.title: Differentiate a reading task for mixed abilities
guides.summary: Turn one text into three reading levels — and matched questions — in about ten minutes.
guides.outcome: Three versions of the same source text pitched at different reading ages, each with comprehension questions, ready to print or paste into your planning.

concept.label: Why AI is good at rewriting — and where it slips

steps.title: Bring your own text
steps.detail: Start from a text you already trust and have the right to use — a passage from a class novel, a vetted article, or one you wrote. Paste it in rather than asking AI to invent facts, which keeps you in control of accuracy.
steps.prompt: Here is a text I use with my Year 5 class. I want to keep the meaning and key vocabulary but produce three reading levels. [paste text]

steps.title: Ask for levels, not just 'easier'
steps.detail: Name the levels you actually teach to. Concrete targets give far better output than 'simplify this'.
steps.prompt: Produce three versions: (1) two years below grade level, (2) at grade level, (3) one year above with richer vocabulary. Keep each to a similar length. Preserve these key terms: [list].

steps.title: Generate matched questions
steps.detail: Ask for questions that work across all three versions so the class can discuss together even when reading different texts.
steps.prompt: Now write four comprehension questions that suit all three versions — two literal, one inferential, one evaluative.

steps.title: Read it back as the teacher
steps.detail: Skim each version for meaning drift, lost nuance, or vocabulary you didn't want changed. This is the step AI cannot do for you.
steps.tip: If a version flattens an idea you care about, paste that sentence back and ask for a revision rather than regenerating the whole thing.

safety.title: Keep students out of the prompt
safety.content: Differentiation is often driven by specific children's needs. Never enter student names, diagnoses, or identifying details into a consumer AI tool. Describe the level you need ('two years below grade'), not the child. The output is a resource — your professional judgement about who receives which version stays offline.

pedagogyNote.content: Offering the same content at multiple entry points is the Universal Design for Learning principle of multiple means of representation. AI makes this practical at classroom scale — but UDL asks you to plan the variation deliberately, not to lower expectations for some students. Keep the thinking demand constant across levels; vary only the access.

verify: Each version actually preserves the meaning, not just shorter sentences
Key vocabulary you flagged survived the rewrite
The 'above level' version genuinely stretches, rather than just adding length
Questions are answerable from every version

guides.title: Write report comments that still sound like you
guides.summary: Move from evidence notes to a consistent, personal first draft — without handing your voice to a machine.
guides.outcome: A first-draft comment for each student that reflects your evidence and your tone, ready for you to personalise and verify.

steps.title: Anonymise your evidence first
steps.detail: Replace every name with a placeholder before anything goes near the tool. Work from achievement against criteria, not from anything that identifies the child.
steps.prompt: I'll give you anonymised notes for 'Student A' against our reporting criteria. Draft a comment in a warm, specific, professional tone. [paste anonymised notes]

steps.title: Teach it your voice once
steps.detail: Paste two or three comments you wrote yourself in previous years. The tool will mirror your sentence length, warmth and structure far better than any style adjective.
steps.prompt: Match the voice of these comments I wrote: [paste 2–3 examples]. Use the same structure: strength, area for growth, next step.

steps.title: Batch, then break the sameness
steps.detail: Generate drafts for several students, then ask the tool to vary openings so the set doesn't read as templated.
steps.prompt: Draft comments for these five anonymised students. Vary the opening sentence of each so they don't all start the same way.

steps.title: Re-personalise every comment
steps.detail: Swap names back in, add the specific moment only you witnessed, and cut anything that isn't true. A comment is a promise to a family — you sign it, not the tool.
steps.tip: If a draft praises something you didn't actually observe, delete it. Plausible is not the same as true.

safety.title: A report is identifying data — protect it
safety.content: Student names, needs, and behaviour combined are sensitive personal information. Keep all of it out of consumer tools: anonymise before, re-personalise after, and never paste a finished report containing real names back in for 'polishing'. Check whether your school provides an enterprise tool with data protections before doing this at scale.

pedagogyNote.content: Hattie's work puts specific, actionable feedback among the highest-impact moves a teacher makes. A report comment is feedback to both student and family — so the 'next step' line matters more than the praise. Use the time AI saves on phrasing to sharpen what each child should do next, which is the part only you can judge.

verify: Every claim is something you actually observed
No real names or identifying details were ever entered
The 'next step' is specific and achievable, not generic
It reads in your voice, not a corporate one

guides.title: Build a rubric from a task sheet
guides.summary: Turn the assignment you've already written into a clear, level-tagged marking rubric.
guides.outcome: A draft rubric whose criteria come from your task and whose level descriptors are written in language students can act on.

steps.title: Feed it the real task
steps.detail: Paste the actual assignment and any standards you mark against. A rubric grown from your task beats a generic one every time.
steps.prompt: Here is the assessment task and the standards it addresses. Draft a rubric with 4 criteria and 4 levels. [paste task + standards]

steps.title: Anchor the levels to thinking, not effort
steps.detail: Ask for descriptors that describe the quality of thinking at each level, so the rubric rewards understanding rather than word count.
steps.prompt: Make the level descriptors describe quality of reasoning and evidence use, not amount of work. Avoid words like 'some', 'good', 'excellent' on their own.

steps.title: Pressure-test the top and bottom
steps.detail: Ask the tool to write a sample response at the highest and lowest level, then check whether your rubric actually distinguishes them.
steps.prompt: Write a short sample response at the top level and one at the bottom level, so I can check the rubric separates them clearly.

steps.title: Mark one real script against it
steps.detail: Run a single piece of genuine student work through your own marking with the draft rubric. Where you hesitate, the descriptor needs work — fix it before you mark the set.

safety.title: Don't outsource the judgement
safety.content: A rubric is a tool for your professional judgement, not a replacement for it. Never paste identifiable student work into a consumer tool to 'auto-mark' it, and treat any AI-suggested grade as a prompt to look more closely yourself — assessment decisions, and responsibility for them, stay with the teacher.

pedagogyNote.content: Webb's Depth of Knowledge helps you check a rubric rewards more than recall. Ask whether your top level requires strategic or extended reasoning (DOK 3–4) rather than just more correct facts (DOK 1). AI can draft descriptors at each level, but you decide what depth the task is really for.

verify: Criteria come from your task, not a generic template
Top and bottom levels are genuinely distinguishable
Descriptors reward thinking, not volume or effort
You could hand it to a student and they'd know what to improve

guides.title: Make a quiz that climbs the thinking levels
guides.summary: Generate formative questions that move beyond recall — fast, and mapped to Bloom's.
guides.outcome: A short formative quiz with questions deliberately spread across thinking levels, plus an answer key you've checked.

concept.label: Why you must check every AI answer key

steps.title: Name the topic and the spread
steps.detail: Tell the tool the exact content and ask for a deliberate spread of question types rather than 'ten questions'.
steps.prompt: Create 8 questions on [topic] for Year 8: 2 remembering, 2 understanding, 2 applying, 2 analysing. Label each with its level.

steps.title: Demand an answer key with reasoning
steps.detail: Ask for worked answers, not just letters, so you can check the tool's reasoning — and reuse it for feedback.
steps.prompt: Provide a full answer key with one line of reasoning per question.

steps.title: Verify every answer yourself
steps.detail: AI is confidently wrong often enough that an unchecked key is a real risk. Work the questions yourself or check against a trusted source before it reaches students.
steps.tip: Maths and anything with a single right answer is where AI most often slips — check those first.

safety.title: Check before it reaches students
safety.content: An AI answer key can be wrong with total confidence. A wrong key marked as correct teaches the error and erodes trust. Always verify questions and answers yourself before use — and if students will use the AI tool directly, make sure it's age-appropriate and approved for them, not just for you.

pedagogyNote.content: Bloom's taxonomy is most useful here as a deliberate spread: a quiz that's all recall tells you who memorised, not who understood. Asking AI to label each question by level makes the cognitive demand visible, so you can see at a glance whether your check actually probes higher-order thinking.

verify: Every answer in the key is actually correct
Questions genuinely sit at the level they're labelled
Language suits the year level you teach
The quiz checks understanding, not just recall

guides.title: Scaffold a writing task without writing it for them
guides.summary: Build frames, model texts and sentence starters that support student writing — without the AI doing the writing.
guides.outcome: A set of scaffolds — a writing frame, a model paragraph, and sentence starters — that help students structure their own writing, ready to hand out or display.

concept.label: Why fluent AI text can fake understanding

steps.title: Describe the task and the students
steps.detail: Tell the tool the writing task, the year level, and where students get stuck. Scaffolds should target the specific hurdle, not replace the writing.
steps.prompt: Year 8 students are writing a persuasive paragraph on [topic]. Many struggle to structure an argument. Create a writing frame with prompts for each part — claim, evidence, reasoning, link.

steps.title: Ask for a model, then mark it up
steps.detail: Get one model paragraph, then have the tool annotate why each sentence works — the annotations are the teaching, not the model itself.
steps.prompt: Write one model paragraph at this level, then annotate each sentence to show its job in the argument.

steps.title: Generate sentence starters, not whole sentences
steps.detail: Starters open a door; finished sentences close it. Ask for openers students complete in their own words.
steps.prompt: Give me eight sentence starters students can finish themselves to express a claim, counter a view, or link evidence.

steps.title: Plan the fade
steps.detail: Decide which scaffolds you'll remove next time. A scaffold that never fades becomes a crutch. This judgement is yours, not the tool's.
steps.tip: Hand out the frame for the first task, the starters for the second, and nothing by the third — make the fade explicit to students.

safety.title: Keep the writing the student's own
safety.content: Scaffolds support writing; they must not produce it. Don't paste a student's draft into a consumer tool to 'finish' or 'improve' it — that replaces their voice and may expose their work. The aim is a student who can write without the scaffold next time.

pedagogyNote.content: Worked examples and frames reduce the extraneous load of figuring out structure, freeing working memory for the actual thinking — the argument. Cognitive Load Theory also warns of the expertise-reversal effect: fade the scaffolds as students gain fluency, or they start to get in the way.

verify: The scaffolds support structure, not supply the content
Sentence starters are genuinely open-ended, not near-complete
The model is at the right level and actually well-written
You know which scaffold you'll remove next time

guides.title: Draft a clear parent update fast
guides.summary: Turn a few bullet points into a warm, clear note home — in your voice, free of jargon.
guides.outcome: A clear, warm draft update for families — newsletter blurb, excursion note or class update — ready for you to check and send.

steps.title: Give it the facts as bullets
steps.detail: List the essentials — what, when, what families need to do. Bullets in, prose out is faster and more accurate than asking it to invent details.
steps.prompt: Turn these bullet points into a warm, clear note to families. Keep it under 150 words and jargon-free. [paste bullets]

steps.title: Set the tone and reading level
steps.detail: Ask for plain language a busy parent can scan, and a tone that matches your school's voice.
steps.prompt: Use a friendly, professional tone at about a Year 6 reading level. Lead with what families need to do.

steps.title: Make a version families can access
steps.detail: Ask for a plain-language or translated version where it helps — a quick win for equity and inclusion.
steps.prompt: Now give me a version in plain English, and a [language] translation for families who need it.

steps.title: Read it as a parent, then send
steps.detail: Check the facts, dates and tone yourself. You are accountable for what goes home, not the tool.
steps.tip: Double-check every date, time and name — these are exactly what AI will cheerfully get wrong.

safety.title: No student details in the prompt
safety.content: Keep individual children out of general communications drafted in consumer tools — no names, needs or incidents. Write the note about the class or event, and handle anything child-specific yourself, through your school's secure channels.

pedagogyNote.content: Strong home–school communication is part of the feedback environment around a learner: clear, timely information helps families support learning. The time AI saves on wording is best reinvested in making the message specific and genuinely useful to families, not just in sending more of them.

verify: Every date, time and detail is correct
It reads in your school's voice, not a generic one
No individual student is identifiable
A busy parent could scan it in under a minute
